coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation of highly thermosensitive N-doped carbon dots and their detection of temperature and 6-mercaotopurine
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Piao
Peng, Huanjun
Zhang, Zilong
Zhang, Zhongying
Chen, Yaping
Chen, Jun
Zhu, Xiaolan
Peng, Jingdong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have sparked immense interest because of their attractive optical properties in recent years, but the synthesis of multifunction CDs is still worth investigating. In this paper, a thermosensitive N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared by facile hydrothermal method with sodium citrate and NH4Cl as raw materials. The N-CDs with a fluorescence quantum yield of 27.4% are excitation-independent, small size (1.34 nm), and good water-soluble. Importantly, the N-CDs display marvelous thermosensitive fluorescence response with good linear relationship, reversibility, and restorability in a range from 15 to 75 ℃, which can be applied to temperature sensing. Detailed characterization show that the thermosensitive fluorescence mechanism was the activation of nonradiative channels in higher temperatures. Besides, based on the inner filter effect (IFE), N-CDs could be served as a fluorescent probe for selective detection of 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) without any modification or functionalization. The simple and efficient strategy for 6-MP sensing was quantified in a linear range of 1–90 µM with a detection limit of 0.86 µM and also can be applied to lake water and serum samples with satisfying recoveries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Thermosensitive
6-Mercaptopurine
Inner filter effect
NH4Cl

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ionic strengths, radiation time, and pH effect on N-CDs
Optimization of experimental parameters for 6-MP detection
Possible fluorescence quenching mechanism for 6-MP detection
Selectivity and interference of N-CDs for the determination of 6-MP

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors. The authors special thanks the Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS and FL lifetimes analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation of highly thermosensitive N-CDs and their detection of temperature and 6-MP.

Morphology and compositions characterization of N-CDs. (a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images, (b) particle size distribution, (c) FT-IR spectra of sodium citrate and N-CDs. (d) Full XPS spectrum. High-resolution XPS data of C 1 s (e), N 1 s (f).

(a) UV–vis and FL spectra of the N-CDs. Inset: photograph under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light. (b) Emission spectra of N-CDs with different excitation wavelengths from 280 nm to 380 nm. (c) 3D fluorescence plots of N-CDs. (d) Fluorescence emission spectra measured from 15 ℃ to 75 ℃ (top to bottom) under the excitation of 340 nm in aqueous solution. (e) Linear fitting curves at the plots of Ln (F0/F) vs. temperature of N-CDs in the processes of heating and re-cooling. (f) The FL decay curves of N-CDs under various temperatures.

The effects of different conditions for 6-MP detection: (a) pH values; (b) the reactive time. (c) The fluorescence spectra of N-CDs (2 µg/mL) with various concentration of 6-MP (from the top to down: 0–150 µM). Inset: variation trend of F0/F as the concentrations of 6-MP are in a range of 0–150 µM. (d) Calibration curve between F0/F and the concentrations of 6-MP (1–90 µM).

(a) FL decay curves of N-CDs and N-CDs mixed with 6-MP. (b) The absorption spectra of 6-MP, N-CDs, and the mixture of 6-MP and N-CDs (c) The absorption spectrum of 6-MP and the excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs. (d) The absorption spectra of the solutions that mixed N-CDs with different concentrations of 6-MP (0–30 µM). Inset: Calibration curve of absorption and concentrations of 6-MP (3–30 µM).
6-MP detection for a comparation of other reported methods.
Recovery test for 6-MP sensing in lake water and human serum.

head.ce:title: Facile preparation of highly thermosensitive N-doped carbon dots and their detection of temperature and 6-mercaotopurine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have sparked immense interest because of their attractive optical properties in recent years, but the synthesis of multifunction CDs is still worth investigating. In this paper, a thermosensitive N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared by facile hydrothermal method with sodium citrate and NH4Cl as raw materials. The N-CDs with a fluorescence quantum yield of 27.4% are excitation-independent, small size (1.34 nm), and good water-soluble. Importantly, the N-CDs display marvelous thermosensitive fluorescence response with good linear relationship, reversibility, and restorability in a range from 15 to 75 ℃, which can be applied to temperature sensing. Detailed characterization show that the thermosensitive fluorescence mechanism was the activation of nonradiative channels in higher temperatures. Besides, based on the inner filter effect (IFE), N-CDs could be served as a fluorescent probe for selective detection of 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) without any modification or functionalization. The simple and efficient strategy for 6-MP sensing was quantified in a linear range of 1–90 µM with a detection limit of 0.86 µM and also can be applied to lake water and serum samples with satisfying recoveries.

ce:section.ce:para: 6-Mercaptopurine (6-MP) is an antagonist to the endogenous purines, which is widely applied to treat childhood acute lymphocytic leukemia and chronic inflammatory diseases [1,2]. It is because that 6-MP restrains the biosynthesis of adenine nucleotides and has the cytotoxicity nature towards various cells, improperly using it can increase the rate of miscarriage during the first trimester of pregnancy and also exhibits serious side effects to human and other organisms [3]. Therefore, it is essential for human and other organisms to monitor the concentration of 6-MP. Some traditional analytical techniques including high-performance liquid chromatography [4], mass spectrometry [5], electrochemical [6], surface enhanced Raman scattering [7], chemiluminescence [8], etc. have been reported to determine 6-MP. However, these technologies are often constrained by the production of organic pollutants, expensive instruments, poor repeatability, low sensitivity, and tedious operating procedures. It is still challenging for the sensing of 6-MP to develop assays with environment-friendly, cost-effectiveness, high sensitivity, and simplicity.

As one of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted immense attention in sensing, catalysis, and bioimaging owing to their excellent photostability, high electron-transfer efficiency, low cytotoxicity, and easy to prepare [9,10]. Though CDs have been described to monitor 6-MP, the modification of fluorophore-labelled ssDNA [11], the combination of metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) [12], or the introduction of Hg2+ [13] increased the complexity or toxicity of these methods. Therefore, a more simple, security and rapid method is called for to monitor the concentration of 6-MP for human and other organisms.

Nowadays, strategies for the synthesis of CDs are generally divided into bottom-up and top-down methods with different raw materials [14]. In these strategies, the doping of heteroatoms plays a significant role in tuning the optical properties and elevating the fluorescent performance [15–17]. Nitrogen is a quintessential dopant because of its five valence electrons and the atomic size similar to carbon. Therefore, nitrogen doping has been most widely used to enhance the performance of CDs [18]. However, nitrogen always comes from organic molecules, such as ethylenediamine [19], urea [20], and phenylenediamine [21]. Ju et al. have reported the strategy for the preparation of multicolor CDs with antibacterial activity by inorganic salt [22], which inspired us to introduce inorganic species (NH4Cl) in CDs for further researches. In this regard, NH4Cl as an inorganic dopant will broaden the choice of dopants for the synthesis of CDs.

Temperature is one of the fundamental parameters of the physical, chemical, and biological systems. The measurement of temperature accurately is of great importance for physiology and biology [23]. So far, plenty of nanomaterials, such as polymers, quantum dots, and organic dyes, based on Raman spectroscopy, fluorescence (FL) emission or lifetimes, have been developed to monitor temperature [24–26]. Nevertheless, compared with the luminescence mechanism and chemical sensors, there are relatively few researches on thermosensitive fluorescent CDs. Although carbon dots for temperature sensing have been reported [27–29], some of them are response to temperature with lower sensitivity. It is quite essential and urgent to develop CDs with high thermal sensitivity for temperature sensing.

In this work, we reported an inorganic salt as a dopant to synthesized thermosensitive N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) through hydrothermal reaction by sodium citrate and NH4Cl for the detection of temperature and 6-MP without any additional modification or functionalization (Fig. 1 ). It was found that the synthesized N-CDs show strong fluorescence, excitation-independent, small size, and superior water-solubility. The as-prepared N-CDs also shown marvelous thermal sensitivity with significant reversibility and recoverability. According to the examination of fluorescence lifetimes, the thermosensitive mechanism was confirmed as the activation of nonradiative channels in higher temperatures. In addition, the system for 6-MP detection was based on the inner filter effect (IFE) with good sensitivity and selectivity, and successfully employed in real samples.
The regents and instruments used in this work were described in the Supporting Information.

N-CDs were prepared through facile one-step hydrothermal method from sodium citrate as carbon source and NH4Cl as nitrogen dopant. Briefly, 588 mg (2 mmol) sodium citrate and 214 mg (4 mmol) NH4Cl were dissolved in 10 mL water. The colorless transparent solution was sealed into a 50 mL Teflon autoclave and reacted at 180 ℃ for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature automatically, the product was filtered through a pore of 0.22 µm filter membrane to remove large particles. Then 25 mL acetone was added into the filtrate to precipitate N-CDs. After washed with acetone for several times, the precipitation was dispersed in 5 mL distilled water and lyophilized to obtain solid powder.

The relative quantum yield was measured by taking quinine sulphate as a standard [30], which was calculated according to the following equation:
(1)
Φ =
Φ S
×
η
2
η
s
2
×
Grad
Grad
s
where Φ corresponds to the quantum yield, S represents standard,
Grad
refers to the slope of integrated FL emission intensity versus absorbance plot of N-CDs, and η denotes the refractive index of the solvent. Five points were measured and the absorption values were kept < 0.05 to avoid self-absorption. N-CDs and quinine sulfate (
Φ S
= 0.54) were dissolved in water ( η = 1.33) and 0.1 M H2SO4 (
η S
= 1.33), respectively.

For the determination of 6-MP, 1 mL Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solution with pH 6.5, 80 µL N-CDs (100 µg/mL) and different volumes of 6-MP (0–100 µL) were added into a 5 mL Eppendorf tube gradually. Then the solution was diluted to tick mark of 4 mL with distilled water to make sure the final concentration of 6-MP ranging from 0 to 150 µM. After 5 min, the FL spectra were recorded at laboratory temperature. The 6-MP was replaced by different interferences for the verification of selectivity.

The typical transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was applied to investigate the microscopic morphology. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the as-prepared N-CDs are uniformly dispersed dot-shape nanoparticles. The diameters of N-CDs (calculated more than 100 dots, Fig. 2b) distributed from 0.9 to 1.9 nm, which are small with an average particle size of 1.34 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset of Fig. 2a) of an individual nanoparticle displays a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which is agree with the (100) lattice spacing of graphene [31] and close to the diffraction facets of graphite [32]. The crystal morphology of the as-prepared N-CDs was measured by X-ray diffraction (XRD). As is demonstrated in Fig. S1, a common broad peak at 2θ = 21.6° can be observed in the XRD pattern, which is assigned to the (002) plane of graphene [20]. Combining the results of HRTEM and XRD, we can infer that graphitization occurred during the formation of N-CDs.

The Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) surveys were taken to further analyze the chemical structures and compositions of as-prepared N-CDs. The FT-IR analysis was displayed in Fig. 2c, these broad absorption bands observed at about 3415 and 3242 cm−1 corresponded to O–H and N–H stretching vibration separately. The peak at 1582 cm-1was ascribed to the asymmetric stretching vibration of carboxylic anions [33], which is consistent with sodium citrate. The characteristic peaks at 1399 and 1077 cm−1 were attributed to the vibration absorption signal of C-N and C-O-C, respectively. The FT-IR absorption curve indicates that there are a lot of functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxylic anions, and amino on the surface of the N-CDs, resulting in a pretty good water-solubility. As seen from the full spectrum of XPS (Fig. 2d), three typical peaks at 284.6 eV, 399.7 eV, 531.1 eV agree with C 1 s, N 1 s, and O1s peak, separately, which proved N-CDs were composed of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. The elements content of C, N, and O were calculated to be 61.86, 4.26, and 33.88%, respectively. In the fitting C 1 s XPS spectra (Fig. 2e), three binding energies of 284.6, 286.2, and 288. 1 eV are in good agreement with C = C/C–C, C-N/C-O, and C = O. From Fig. 2f, the N 1 s spectra can be deconvoluted into peaks at 399.7 and 401.2 eV, which correspond to C-N and N–H, respectively. O 1 s XPS spectra (Fig. S2) showed three different sub-peaks at 531.0, 532.4, and 535.3 eV can be assigned to CO, C-O and O-CO. The experimental results of XPS further confirmed the analysis of FT-IR. Taken together these results of FT-IR and XPS, nitrogen was doped in N-CDs successfully, and nitrogen existed in C-N and N–H mainly.

To evaluate the optical properties, UV–vis absorption and FL spectra of the as-prepared N-CDs aqueous solution were characterized. Firstly, the N-CDs display bright blue fluorescence under a irradiation of UV light at 365 nm (inset of Fig. 3 a). The quantum yield was calculated to be about 27.4% (Fig. S3) by taking quinine sulphate as a standard. From the UV–vis spectrum of N-CDs in Fig. 3a, there are two absorption bands at 234 nm and 334 nm, which are likely due to π-π* transition of the CC bond and n-π* transition of the CO bond [34], separately. In addition, the maximum emission peak at 440 nm was obtained under 340 nm excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. 3b, these FL emission spectra of N-CDs under different excitation were also recorded. The emission peak at 440 nm remains unchanged with the excitation wavelength changed from 280 to 380 nm, which exhibited an excitation-independent photoluminescence property. The excitation-independent optical property can be attributed to the molecular fluorophores on the surface or embedded in the CDs matrix [35]. Part of the molecular fluorophores were retained as acetone was used to precipitate the N-CDs in the purification process. From the 3D spectra of N-CDs in Fig. 3c, it is also can be seen that the emission peak does not vary with the excitation and there is a single emission center located at 440 nm. The single emission center also can be verified by the following monoexponential decay of fluorescence lifetime. As the results above, 340 nm was selected as the optimum excitation wavelength for further experiments.

It was interesting to note that the fluorescence emission of N-CDs exhibited thermosensitive, which was quenched by heating and restored after cooling (Fig. S4). The effect of temperature from 15 to 75 ℃ on the FL intensity was tested on the fluorescence spectrometer with a circulating water bath. As illustrated in Fig. 3d, the fluorescence signals reduced gradually with the temperature increased under the excitation of 340 nm. The logarithm of FL intensity ratio (F0/F) has an excellent linear correlation with temperature and the linear regressive curve can be expressed as:
(2)
Ln
F 0
/ F
= 0.0186 T - 0.3218
R 2
= 0.9947 ,
T : 15 - 75
° C

In the formula above, T refers to the temperature, F0 and F represent the FL intensity at 15 and T℃ separately. It was clearly observed in Fig. 3e, the FL was reversible with the temperature back to 15 ℃, and the re-cooling also shows good linear relationship. Importantly, the FL intensity decreased by 67% with the temperature increased to 75 ℃. The thermal sensitivity of N-CD was calculated to be 1.12% ℃-1, which shows a higher sensitivity to temperature by comparing with some published reports in Table S1. Additionally, the thermosensitive fluorescence response showed a remarkable reversibility and restorability when turned temperatures from 15 to 75 ℃ for 6 cycles (Fig. S5). These characteristics indicate that the N-CDs possess great potential for temperature sensing.

Though some thermosensitive CDs have been explored, the thermosensitive mechanism of fluorescent CDs was still no unanimous conclusion. The TEM and fluorescence lifetimes under different temperatures were measured to understand the thermosensitive mechanism. As shown in Fig S6, the average diameter of N-CDs (calculated 100 dots) under 15, 45, 75 ℃ are approximately 2.26, 2.20 and 2.12 nm, respectively. No obvious size changes can be observed, indicating that the fluorescence quenching was not caused by aggregation. It is depicted that the FL lifetimes decreased from 9.42 to 7.37 ns with the temperature increased from 15 to 75 ℃ in Fig. 3f. The relaxion dynamics becomes faster, which can be attributed to the occurrence of nonradiative decay process. Some nonradiative channels, including defect and ionized impurity states, were activated at higher temperatures, and provided more chance for excited electrons to return to the ground state by the nonradiative process, which results in the decrease of FL intensity [36,37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ionic strengths, radiation time, and pH effect on N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of salt concentrations on the FL emission was also examined. As a result, the FL intensity still unchanged though the concentration of NaCl reached up to 1.0 mol L-1 in Fig. S7, which indicated an excellent salt-tolerance of the N-CDs. The photostability of N-CDs was further examined with the irradiation of 340 nm excitation. As can be seen from Fig. S8, as the radiation time increased, the FL intensity of N-CDs decreased slightly but still keep 72% (greater than65%) after an hour, indicating a relatively good photostability [38]. In addition, the effect of pH on N-CDs was tested in BR buffer solution with distinct pH. As showed in Fig. S9, the FL intensity increased gradually as pH ranged from 2.0 to 6.0 and remains unchanged at higher pH (6.0–11.0). The phenomenon could be attributed to the protonation and deprotonation of carboxyl groups on the surface of N-CDs. The carboxyl groups protonated in lower pH, which will give rise to the aggregation of N-CDs and the FL intensity decreased. In higher pH, the carboxyl groups deprotonated, that N-CDs were dispersed well and the FL intensity is almost constant [39,40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental parameters for 6-MP detection
ce:section.ce:para: The FL of N-CDs was quenched when 6-MP appeared. To get relatively high sensitivity and broad linear range, the effects of reaction pH and times were investigated. Fig. 4 a shows the effect of solution pH on the FL intensity before and after the addition of 6-MP. The declination of the mixture is most obviously when pH was 6.5 and the FL intensity was also stable for the sensing strategy. Thus, the optimal pH value was set at 6.5 for subsequent researches. The effect of reaction time in 0–30 min was investigated. The fluorescence intensity was decreased quickly from 0 to 2 min, and then keep stable 5 min later (Fig. 4b). As mentioned earlier, the FL of as-prepared N-CDs reduced with an increasing of temperature of the system. Therefore, after adding 6-MP for 5 min, the FL intensity was recorded under room temperature to keep the signal stable.

Based on the FL quenching of N-CDs, the FL emission intensity of the solution with different concentrations of 6-MP at 440 nm was recorded under optimum conditions discussed above. As shown in Fig. 4c, the FL intensity declined gradually with 6-MP concentration increasing from 0 to 150 μM. There is a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9953) between the ratio of FL intensity (F0/F, where F0 and F refer the FL intensities with and without 6-MP respectively) and the concentrations of 6-MP with a range of 1–90 μM in Fig. 4d. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.86 μM (LOD = 3σ/S, σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal (n = 5) and S is the slope of the calibration plot). The sensing strategy constructed in our study exhibited a wider linear range or lower LOD for a comparation of those researches reported previously in Table 1 . Moreover, the N-CDs is facile to prepare with simple precursors and no embellishment or functionalization is required. The detection system is fast, sensitive and does not require other auxiliary substances or complex nanomaterials, which manifest significant advantages for the monitor of 6-MP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible fluorescence quenching mechanism for 6-MP detection
ce:section.ce:para: It is reported that those feasible quenching mechanisms of the fluorescent CDs as a probe mainly contain the inner filter effect, static/dynamic quenching, and electron/energy transfer [48]. To understand the mechanism of N-CDs for 6-MP detection, some experiments were conducted. Firstly, the fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs with and without the addition of 6-MP were examined. As demonstrated in Fig. 5 a, there was no obvious fluctuation in FL lifetimes of N-CDs (τ0 = 8.13 ns) and with the addition of 6-MP (τ = 8.12 ns), it means that there was neither dynamic quenching nor electron/energy transfer between N-CDs and 6-MP, because the FL lifetime of N-CDs will reduce if the two mechanisms exist [49]. It is known that static quenching occurs when the carbon dots interacted with analyte, which formed a new compound and impaired the absorption spectrum [50]. So the UV–vis of 6-MP, N-CDs, and the mixture of 6-MP and N-CDs were examined. As illustrated in Fig. 5b, no new absorption bands appeared in the UV–vis of the mixture of N-CDs and 6-MP, which is consist with the superposition data, indicating that there is no new compound formation between N-CDs and 6-MP and static quenching did not occur. In addition, the FT-IR spectra were recorded to explore whether N-CDs binding with 6-MP. It can be seen from Fig. S10 that the FT-IR absorption did not change significantly before and after the addition of 6-MP, implying N-CDs did not bind with 6-MP. The results of FT-IR and UV–vis analysis are consistent, which further illustrates that static quenching has not occurred. IFE occurs when the absorption spectrum of the analyte overlapped with the fluorescence excitation spectra of CDs [51]. Apparently, the absorption of spectrum of 6-MP shows a good overlap with the fluorescence excitation spectrum of N-CDs in Fig. 5c, which meets the conditions for the occurrence of IFE. Because of the competition of absorption, the excitation of N-CDs was shielded, inducing the decline of FL intensity.

To further verified the process of IFE between N-CDs and 6-MP, the UV–vis absorption spectra of drugs with similar structure to 6-MP, including hypoxanthine (Hyp), adenine (Ade), guanine (Gua) were displayed in Fig. S11. It can be seen that only the absorption of 6-MP overlaps with the excitation spectrum of N-CDs, while other analogues almost do not overlap, so that the competing absorption and excitation light shielding do not occur. Besides, from the absorption spectra of the solutions that mixed N-CDs with different concentrations of 6-MP (Fig. 5d), the UV–vis absorption increased gradually and displayed a good linear correlation at 340 nm. It means that as the increase of competitive absorption, the continuous addition of 6-MP makes the fluorescence of the system decrease regularly. These results demonstrated the quenching process of the IFE again in the system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference of N-CDs for the determination of 6-MP
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the proposed method, we measured the FL response of the N-CDs by replacing 6-MP with a range of possible interfering substances (90 µM). As displayed in Fig. S12, some general mental ions such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+ and various small organic molecules including ascorbic acid (AA), glycine (Gly), lysine (Lys), glucose (Glu), saccharose (Sac), L-cysteine (Cys), and some other drugs with a similar structure have no significant signal response to the FL of N-CDs. Besides, the mixture of these interferences with 6-MP also did not interfere with the sensing of 6-MP. All these results demonstrated that the developed sensing platform has good selectivity and anti-interference ability for 6-MP.

The accuracy of the sensing strategy is further evaluated by a common standard addition method. 6-MP with different concentrations (0, 3.00, 45.00, 90.00) were spiked into lake water and human serum samples. The collected lake water from our campus was boiled to remove the microorganisms, and then filtered via 0.22 μm membrane filters and adjusted the pH to 7.0. Different concentrations of 6-MP were injected into solutions with 10% water samples for analysis. As indicated in Table 2 , the recoveries for lake water were between 95.8% and 101.8% with a relative standard deviation of 1.4–3.1%. The serum sample obtained from healthy people were diluted 200-fold for analysis. The recoveries in serum were in a range of 91.8–102.1% with a relative standard deviation of 1.5–4.3%. The satisfactory results suggested that the developed method with good accuracy and reliability is a promising sensor platform for 6-MP.

In summary, the novel N-CDs with an inorganic salt as a dopant from sodium citrate and NH4Cl by a facile and environmental hydrothermal method was developed. The as-prepared N-CDs display superior optical properties, small size, and good water-solubility. More strikingly, N-CDs exhibit remarkable thermosensitive in a range of 15–75 ℃ with good linearity, reversibility, and recoverability. From the results of the FL lifetime, the thermosensitive fluorescent response mechanism was confirmed to be the activation of nonradiative channels in higher temperatures. Furthermore, because of the IFE between N-CDs and 6-MP, N-CDs can be applied to rapid and selective detection of 6-MP without any modification or functionalization. The sensitive detection platform for 6-MP sensing successfully applied to water and human serum samples. Our work can provide a novel approach for the preparation of highly thermosensitive N-CDs from simple and inexpensive raw materials and the N-CDs with superior properties has a broad prospect in the detection of temperature and 6-MP.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors. The authors special thanks the Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS and FL lifetimes analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106835.
